title: Foundation/Makeup on Silk
stainChemistry: Liquid foundation contains pigment (iron oxides, titanium dioxide), oils (silicone, mineral oil), waxes, and emulsifiers. On silk, the oil component penetrates the fiber while pigment particles lodge in the surface texture. Water-based foundations are easier to remove than oil-based, but both contain pigment that can stain. Setting powders and primers add additional polymer layers.
whyThisWorks: Solvent or POG dissolves the oil/silicone matrix on silk, releasing the trapped pigment particles. Surfactant then lifts the liberated pigment. For stubborn mineral pigment residue, a mild oxidizer addresses remaining discoloration. Working gently is key — aggressive rubbing drives pigment particles deeper into the fiber structure.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply POG to dissolve the oil/wax component — 5-10 min dwell time
2. Apply NSD to lift dissolved residue and pigment — 3-5 min dwell time
3. If color remains: apply sodium hydrosulfite on colorfast fabrics — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min
warnings: No peroxide on silk — use sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) instead

steps: 1. Scrape or blot excess — do NOT rub (spreading worsens it)
2. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat Dawn application until stain fades
4. Rinse with warm water (unless protein component present — then cold)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 rounds of treatment. Combo stains are inherently harder — a professional cleaner has specialized chemistry for each component
warnings: Treat each component in order — don't skip steps
Cold water always — heat sets protein components permanently

neverDo: No protease enzymes — silk IS protein; enzymes digest the fiber
No chlorine bleach — dissolves protein fibers
No alkaline detergents (pH >8) — breaks peptide bonds
No acetone — bleaches and damages fiber structure
fiberSensitivities: Protein fiber — extremely sensitive to enzymes, alkali, and heat
Dyes bleed easily — always test first

escalation.when: If makeup stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is makeup on silk, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Can you identify the specific components?
Is there a greasy/oily feel to the stain?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
